  • Hide All Code

What Makes an Airbnb Premium? Machine Learning Analysis of Melbourne Listings

Joel John Kurien (540666979) | Qilong Zou (550542832) | Subhamay Ghosh (550535650)) | Victor Kibisu Alusa (540921269) | Wengan Li (510010137)

November 01, 2025

1 Problem Definition

1.1 Research question

To what extent can machine learning identify the key differentiators between Budget, Standard, and Premium Airbnb listings in Melbourne Australia, and which property and host characteristics are the most influential drivers of this classification?

This project is a multiclass classification problem. We are predicting a target variable, price_tier, which has three discrete categories: Budget, Standard and Premium. The goal of our model is to learn the patterns from the various features of the listings and then assign a new, unseen listing to one of these three predefined classes. This is fundamentally different from a regression problem, where we would be predicting the exact dollar price.

1.2 Why does it matter, and how does it connect to Sydney/Australia?

This investigation has significant practical relevance for hosts, guests, and policymakers in major Australian tourism hubs like Melbourne and Sydney. By simplifying the price structure into three intuitive tiers, the insights become far more actionable:

For Hosts and Property Investors: For a host in a competitive market like Sydney, understanding what separates a ‘Standard’ property from a ‘Premium’ one is crucial. Our model can highlight which specific features (e.g., professional photos, a dedicated workspace, superhost status) provide the best return on investment to move a listing into a higher price tier.

For Consumers (Travelers): The classification provides clarity for tourists. It helps them understand the value proposition of listings in cities like Sydney and Melbourne, allowing them to easily filter for properties that match their budget and expectations for quality.

For Urban Planning and Market Analysis: For local councils and tourism bodies, analyzing the distribution and characteristics of ‘Budget’ versus ‘Premium’ listings can reveal patterns about neighbourhood development, gentrification, and the overall health of the short-term rental market. This is a key issue in Sydney, where the impact of Airbnb on housing affordability is a constant topic of debate.

Revenue optimisation: Sydney hosts face the nation’s sharpest occupancy decline (-12 % YoY, AirDNA 2024). A reliable Budget/Standard/Premium flag lets new hosts benchmark against comparable stock and adjust pricing or amenities quickly.

2 Data Description

2.1 Data Sources

  • Primary Dataset: Melbourne Airbnb listings, obtained from OpenML (OpenML, n.d.) (download: Kaggle (TylerX, n.d.), original source: insideairbnb (Inside Airbnb, n.d.))

  • Publication: (Shi et al., 2021) benchmarked multimodal AutoML methods for tabular data with text fields.

2.2 Data Composition

# -------------------------------
# Load dataset
# -------------------------------
airbnb_data <- read.arff("dataset_")

# Convert character columns to factors
char_cols <- sapply(airbnb_data, is.character)
airbnb_data[char_cols] <- lapply(airbnb_data[char_cols], as.factor)

# Ensure target variable is factor
if ("price_label" %in% names(airbnb_data)) {
  airbnb_data$price_label <- as.factor(airbnb_data$price_label)
}

airbnb_data <- airbnb_data %>% select(-c(space, description, summary))

# Display as interactive scrollable table
datatable(
  airbnb_data[1:5,],
  options = list(
    pageLength = 1,       # show 1 rows per page
    scrollX = TRUE,        # horizontal scroll
    scrollY = "100px"      # vertical scroll with fixed height
  )
)
# -------------------------------
# Missingness plot (top 10 cols)
# -------------------------------
total_missing <- sum(is.na(airbnb_data))
missing_top10 <- sort(colSums(is.na(airbnb_data)), decreasing = TRUE)[1:10]

missing_df <- data.frame(
  Column = names(missing_top10),
  Missing = as.numeric(missing_top10)
)

missingness_plot <- ggplot(missing_df, aes(x = reorder(Column, Missing), y = Missing)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#E69F00") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Top 10 Columns with Missing Values",
    x = "",
    y = "Number of Missing Values"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    panel.grid = element_blank(),       # removes all grid lines
    panel.background = element_blank(), # removes background
    axis.line = element_line(color = "black"),  # adds clean axes lines
    axis.ticks = element_line(color = "black")  # show ticks
  )

# -------------------------------
# Target Histogram
# -------------------------------
target_histogram <- ggplot(airbnb_data, aes(x = price_label)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "#E69F00") +
  geom_text(stat='count', aes(label=..count..), vjust=1, size=3.5, color = "white") +
  labs(
    title = "Target Class Distribution",
    x = "Price Category",
    y = ""
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    panel.grid = element_blank(),       # removes all grid lines
    panel.background = element_blank(), # removes background
    axis.line = element_line(color = "black"),  # adds clean axes lines
    axis.ticks = element_line(color = "black")  # show ticks
  )

# -------------------------------
# Outliers
# -------------------------------

# Count outliers per numeric column
outliers_summary <- airbnb_data %>%
  select(where(is.numeric)) %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(abs(scale(.)) > 3, na.rm = TRUE))) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Column", values_to = "Outliers")

# ---- Outliers Plot ----
plot_outliers <- outliers_summary %>%
  filter(Column != "TOTAL") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(Column, Outliers), y = Outliers)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#E69F00") +
  coord_flip() +
  geom_text(aes(label = Outliers), hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Outliers per Numeric Column",
    x = "",
    y = "Number of Outliers"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    panel.grid = element_blank(),       # removes all grid lines
    panel.background = element_blank(), # removes background
    axis.line = element_line(color = "black"),  # adds clean axes lines
    axis.ticks = element_line(color = "black")  # show ticks
  )
# -------------------------------
# Summary statistics
# -------------------------------
num_samples <- nrow(airbnb_data)
num_features <- ncol(airbnb_data) - 1  # exclude target
num_price_categories <- length(unique(airbnb_data$price_label))
num_numeric <- sum(sapply(airbnb_data, is.numeric))
num_factor <- sum(sapply(airbnb_data, is.factor))
total_duplicates <- sum(duplicated(airbnb_data))

glue::glue(
  "The dataset has {num_samples} samples, {num_features} features, 
   {num_price_categories} price categories, {num_numeric} numeric and 
   {num_factor} factor columns. The count of duplicate rows is: {total_duplicates}"
)

The dataset has 18316 samples, 100 features, 10 price categories, 32 numeric and 69 factor columns. The count of duplicate rows is: 0

# ---- Combine Plots ----
#plot_outliers + missingness_plot / target_histogram

2.3 Target Class

The target class “price_label” (remapped to price_tier) is a categorical variable with 10 different price levels (“0” through “9”). The dataset can thus be used for multi-class classification.

2.4 Dataset Complexity Analysis

Our dataset meets four of the difficulty criteria: Large size (>10,000 observations), Messy (substantial missing values like license and monthly_price), Complex (>100 features with mixed data types), and Multi-class classification (10 price categories), all of which require careful pre-processing and feature engineering for robust modeling.

3 Data Cleaning & Preparation:

This section preserves as much data as possible while ensuring reliability, maintaining a clean and representative dataset for downstream analysis

3.1 Data Preprocessing Pipeline

To prepare the data for modeling, we implemented a comprehensive preprocessing pipeline designed to clean, standardize, and enrich the raw dataset.The primary objective was to create a clean, leakage-free dataset suitable for predicting price tier (Budget/Standard/Premium) while maintaining data integrity and maximizing predictive power. Initially, we removed non-predictive columns such as IDs, URLs, and geographic coordinates to prevent data leakage and reduce noise. We then standardized various missing value representations (e.g., "?", "") into a consistent NA format and corrected data types, converting text-based flags ("t"/"f") to logical TRUE/FALSE values and ensuring fields representing prices, reviews, and counts were numeric. A critical step in our preparation was the transformation of the target variable: we engineered a new three-tier target—Budget, Standard, Premium—by grouping the original ten price_label categories. This simplification was crucial because the three-tier system aligns far better with real-world consumer behavior, where users think in terms of general price categories rather than ten granular, less intuitive levels. This approach not only aids in creating clearer class balance, clearer visualizations, and direct alignment with policy levers such as NSW’s tiered levy scheme but also simplifies the classification task for our models.

clean <- function(df) {
  
  # no variance, unrelated, redundant columns
  unhelpful_cols <- c(
     "listing_url", "scrape_id", "last_scraped", "calendar_last_scraped", "picture_url",
     "host_id", "name", "host_url", "host_name", "host_thumbnail_url", "host_picture_url", "id",
     "host_location", "host_neighborhood", "host_id", "host_about", "host_verifications",
     "transit", "access", "interaction", "house_rules", "license", "state", "zipcode","city",
     "country", "country_code", "smart_location", "weekly_price", "monthly_price", "has_availability",
     "street", "suburb", "latitude", "longitude", "first_review", "last_review",
     "summary", "space", "description", "neighborhood_overview", "notes")
   df <- df %>% select(-all_of(unhelpful_cols))
   
   
  # replace placeholders with NA
  placeholders <- c("?", "", "#NAME?")
  df[] <- data.frame(lapply(df, function(col) {
    if (is.factor(col)) col <- as.character(col)
    col[col %in% placeholders] <- NA_character_
    col
  }), stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
  

  # Remove quotes on all character/factor columns
  char_cols <- sapply(df, function(x) is.character(x) || is.factor(x))
  df[char_cols] <- lapply(
    df[char_cols],
    function(x) gsub('^"+|"+$', '', as.character(x))
  )
  
  
  # convert to numeric class
  num_cols <- c("accommodates", "bathrooms", "bedrooms", "beds", "security_deposit",
               "cleaning_fee", "guests_included", "extra_people", "minimum_nights",
               "maximum_nights", "availability_30", "availability_60", "availability_90",
               "availability_365", "number_of_reviews", "review_scores_rating",
               "review_scores_accuracy", "review_scores_cleanliness", "review_scores_checkin",
               "review_scores_communication", "review_scores_location", "review_scores_value",
               "calculated_host_listings_count", "review_per_month")
  for (col in num_cols) {
    if (col %in% names(df)) {df[[col]] <- as.numeric(df[[col]])}
  }

  
  
  # Host response rate
  df$host_response_rate <- ifelse(
    is.na(df$host_response_rate),
    NA_real_,
    as.numeric(gsub("%", "", df$host_response_rate))
  )
  
  

  # Standardize host_response_time
  df$host_response_time_missing <- is.na(df$host_response_time) # flag NA rows
  df$host_response_time <- tolower(trimws(df$host_response_time))  # clean whitespace & lowercase
  df$host_response_time <- case_when(
    grepl("within an hour", df$host_response_time) ~ "within an hour",
    grepl("within a few hours", df$host_response_time) ~ "within a few hours",
    grepl("within a day|a few days or more|a day or more", df$host_response_time) ~ "a day or more",
    TRUE ~ 'unknown' # for unexpected/missing values
  )
  
  
  
  # convert t/f to boolean
  bool_cols <- c("host_is_superhost", "host_has_profile_pic", "host_identity_verified",
                 "requires_license", "instant_bookable", "require_guest_profile_picture",
                 "require_guest_phone_verification", "is_location_exact")
  
  for (col in bool_cols) {
    df[[col]] <- df[[col]] == "t"
  }

  
  # Create new labels for the price category & drop price_label, price
  df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    price_tier  = case_when(
      price_label %in% c("0","1","2")     ~ "Budget",
      price_label %in% c("3","4","5","6") ~ "Standard",
      price_label %in% c("7","8","9")     ~ "Premium"
    ),
    price_tier = factor(price_tier, levels = c("Budget","Standard","Premium"))
  ) %>%
  select(-c(price))
  
  
  return(df)
}






# --- Helpers ---------------------------------------------------------------

# Safe statistical mode (returns a single value, preserving type if possible)
.get_mode_value <- function(x) {
  x <- x[!is.na(x)]
  # treat empty strings as missing for character
  if (is.character(x)) x <- x[nzchar(x)]
  if (!length(x)) return(NA)
  ux <- unique(x)
  ux[which.max(tabulate(match(x, ux)))]
}

# Compute medians (numeric only) from a data frame
compute_train_medians <- function(df) {
  num_cols <- vapply(df, is.numeric, logical(1))
  out <- vapply(df[num_cols], function(z) median(z, na.rm = TRUE), numeric(1))
  # named numeric vector
  out
}

# Compute modes (character/factor/logical) from a data frame
compute_train_modes <- function(df) {
  out <- vector("list", length(df))
  names(out) <- names(df)
  for (nm in names(df)) {
    x <- df[[nm]]
    if (is.numeric(x)) next
    out[[nm]] <- .get_mode_value(x)
  }
  # named list; entries are scalars (possibly NA) for non-numeric cols
  out
}

# Impute using training-set medians/modes
imputation <- function(df, medians, modes) {
  for (nm in names(df)) {
    x <- df[[nm]]

    if (is.numeric(x)) {
      med <- medians[[nm]]
      if (!is.null(med) && !is.na(med)) {
        x[is.na(x)] <- med
      }
      df[[nm]] <- x
      next
    }

    # For non-numeric: use precomputed mode (may be NA if no data in train)
    mode_val <- modes[[nm]]

    if (is.logical(x)) {
      # default to FALSE if mode is NA (optional; adjust if you prefer keep-NA)
      if (is.null(mode_val) || is.na(mode_val)) mode_val <- FALSE
      x[is.na(x)] <- as.logical(mode_val)
      df[[nm]] <- x
      next
    }

    if (is.factor(x)) {
      mv_chr <- if (is.null(mode_val) || is.na(mode_val)) NA_character_ else as.character(mode_val)
      # treat empty string as missing as well
      miss <- is.na(x) | (!is.na(x) & as.character(x) == "")
      if (!is.na(mv_chr) && !(mv_chr %in% levels(x))) {
        levels(x) <- c(levels(x), mv_chr)
      }
      x[miss] <- mv_chr
      df[[nm]] <- factor(x, levels = levels(x))
      next
    }

    if (is.character(x)) {
      mv_chr <- if (is.null(mode_val) || is.na(mode_val)) "Unknown" else as.character(mode_val)
      miss <- is.na(x) | x == ""
      x[miss] <- mv_chr
      df[[nm]] <- x
      next
    }

    # Any other types pass through unchanged
  }
  df
}











feature_engineering <- function(df) {
  
  clean_text <- function(x) {
    x <- stri_trans_tolower(x)
    x <- stri_replace_all_regex(x, "\\s+", " ")
    stri_trim_both(x)
  }


  
  # ------------- OHE --------------
  
  # host_response_time ohe
  hrt_vals <- c("within an hour", "within a few hours", "a day or more", "unknown")
  for (val in hrt_vals) {
    df[[paste0("hrt_", val)]] <- df$host_response_time == val
  }

  
  # cancellation_policy ohe
  cancel_policies <- c("flexible", "strict_14_with_grace_period", "moderate",
                       "super_strict_30", "super_strict_60", "strict")
  for (p in cancel_policies) {
    df[[glue("cancel_{p}")]] <- df$cancellation_policy == p
  }
  df$cancellation_policy <- NULL # drop column after ohe

  # property_type ohe
  property_types <- c(
    "Apartment", "Condominium", "House", "Other",
    "Serviced apartment", "Townhouse"
  )
  for (t in property_types) {
    colname <- paste0("property_", make.names(t))
    df[[colname]] <- df$property_type == t
  }
  df$property_type <- NULL  # drop column after ohe
  
  #room_type ohe
  room_types <- c("Entire home/apt", "Private room", "Shared room")
  for (t in room_types) {
    colname <- paste0("room_", make.names(t))
    df[[colname]] <- df$room_type == t
  }
  df$room_type <- NULL  # drop column after ohe

  
  
  # calendar_update to days
  to_days <- function(x) {
    if (is.na(x) || x == "") return(NA_integer_)
    
    x <- tolower(trimws(x))
    
    if (x == "today") return(0)
    if (x == "yesterday") return(1)
    
    # "a day/week/month/year ago"
    if (str_detect(x, "^a\\s+day")) return(1)
    if (str_detect(x, "^a\\s+week")) return(7)
    if (str_detect(x, "^a\\s+month")) return(30)
    if (str_detect(x, "^a\\s+year")) return(365)
    
    # numeric cases (e.g. "2 weeks ago")
    if (str_detect(x, "day"))   return(suppressWarnings(as.numeric(str_extract(x, "\\d+"))))
    if (str_detect(x, "week"))  return(suppressWarnings(as.numeric(str_extract(x, "\\d+"))) * 7)
    if (str_detect(x, "month")) return(suppressWarnings(as.numeric(str_extract(x, "\\d+"))) * 30)
    if (str_detect(x, "year"))  return(suppressWarnings(as.numeric(str_extract(x, "\\d+"))) * 365)
    return(NA_integer_) # never updated → NA
  }
  df$E_calendar_updated_days <- sapply(df$calendar_updated, to_days)
  # create a flag column for never updated and impute using median
  df$E_calendar_updated_days_missing <- is.na(df$E_calendar_updated_days)
  df$E_calendar_updated_days[is.na(df$E_calendar_updated_days)] <- median(df$E_calendar_updated_days, na.rm=TRUE)
  df$calendar_updated <- NULL # drop column after
  
  
  return(df)
}


# 1) Clean first
airbnb_clean <- clean(airbnb_data)

3.2 Missing Data Analysis

3.2.1 Visualization of missing data

unclean_airbnb_df = read.csv("airbnb_data.csv")
airbnb_df <- read.csv("dataset_cleaned_2.csv")
unclean_airbnb_df$price_tier <- as.factor(unclean_airbnb_df$price_tier)
high_missing <- unclean_airbnb_df %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), ~mean(is.na(.)))) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(),
               names_to = "feature",
               values_to = "missing_pct") %>%
  filter(missing_pct > 0.25) %>%
  pull(feature)

# Plot vis_miss only for those features
vis_miss(unclean_airbnb_df[high_missing]) +
  labs(title = "Missing Value Patterns (Features >25% Missing)") +
  theme(legend.position = "right")

- This visualization shows the large numbers of features that have more than 25% of values missing, this implies that more than 25% of the information related to that feature is lost and cannot be easily imputed with other values such as mean/median or mode. This suggests that these features should either be removed or remove the rows that miss values in these attributes. But as the records may hold valuable information about other features in the dataset, it is preferrable that we delete the attributes instead as due to the number of missing values implies that they may not contribute greatly to the classification model. - In the cleaned dataset is we try to completely remove/replace missing values by being imputed by the median of the numerical features and the mode for the nominal/ordinal features. Here, we also observe that the columns that were greatly missing values as shown in the previous graph has been dropped due to the assumption that they will not provide any useful information that is not artifically added by us if we simply imputed the data.

3.3 Feature Engineering

# REMOVING LEAKAGE FEATURES 

leakage_features <- c(
  # Direct price variables - CRITICAL LEAKAGE
  "price_label", "weekly_price", "monthly_price",
  
  # Post-listing review metrics - don't exist for new listings
  "number_of_reviews", "first_review", "last_review",
  "reviews_per_month",
  "review_scores_rating", "review_scores_accuracy",
  "review_scores_cleanliness", "review_scores_checkin",
  "review_scores_communication", "review_scores_location",
  "review_scores_value",  # Especially this - compares value to price!
  
  # Availability/booking outcomes - results of pricing strategy
   "calendar_last_scraped",
  
  # Administrative/metadata - not predictive
  "id", "listing_url", "scrape_id", "last_scraped",
  "host_id", "host_url", "picture_url",
  "host_thumbnail_url", "host_picture_url",
  "name", "neighborhood_overview", "notes",
  "transit", "access", "interaction", "house_rules",
  
  # Geographic coordinates - prevent overfitting to exact locations
  "latitude", "longitude", "smart_location", 
  "street", "city", "state", "zipcode", "country", "country_code",
  
  # License info - mostly missing and not predictive
  "license", "requires_license"
)

# Remove leakage features
airbnb_clean <- airbnb_clean %>%
  select(-any_of(leakage_features))

#############################################################################
# Check cardinality of character variables
high_cardinality <- airbnb_clean %>%
  select(where(is.character)) %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), n_distinct)) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "n_unique") %>%
  filter(n_unique > 50)
high_cardinality


# STEP 3: PARSE AMENITIES INTO BINARY FEATURES
# ==========================================================================

# Function to safely detect amenities
detect_amenity <- function(amenities_col, amenity_name) {
  stringr::str_detect(amenities_col, stringr::fixed(amenity_name))
}

# Define key amenities that differentiate price tiers
# Focus is on structural/permanent features, not operational decisions
airbnb_clean <- airbnb_clean %>%
  mutate(
    # Essential amenities (common across tiers)
    has_wifi = detect_amenity(amenities, "Wifi"),
    has_kitchen = detect_amenity(amenities, "Kitchen"),
    has_tv = detect_amenity(amenities, "TV"),
    has_heating = detect_amenity(amenities, "Heating"),
    has_air_conditioning = detect_amenity(amenities, "Air conditioning"),
    has_washer = detect_amenity(amenities, "Washer"),
    has_dryer = detect_amenity(amenities, "Dryer"),
    
    # Premium amenities (differentiate higher tiers)
    has_pool = detect_amenity(amenities, "Pool"),
    has_hot_tub = detect_amenity(amenities, "Hot tub"),
    has_gym = detect_amenity(amenities, "Gym"),
    has_elevator = detect_amenity(amenities, "Elevator"),
    has_indoor_fireplace = detect_amenity(amenities, "Indoor fireplace"),
    has_waterfront = detect_amenity(amenities, "Waterfront"),
    has_beachfront = detect_amenity(amenities, "Beachfront"),
    
    # Parking (important in Melbourne)
    has_free_parking = detect_amenity(amenities, "Free parking on premises"),
    has_street_parking = detect_amenity(amenities, "Free street parking"),
    has_paid_parking = detect_amenity(amenities, "Paid parking off premises"),
    
    # Work amenities (increasingly important)
    has_workspace = detect_amenity(amenities, "Laptop friendly workspace"),
    has_dedicated_workspace = detect_amenity(amenities, "Dedicated workspace"),
    
    # Safety features (might indicate professional hosts)
    has_smoke_detector = detect_amenity(amenities, "Smoke detector"),
    has_carbon_monoxide = detect_amenity(amenities, "Carbon monoxide detector"),
    has_fire_extinguisher = detect_amenity(amenities, "Fire extinguisher"),
    has_first_aid = detect_amenity(amenities, "First aid kit"),
    
    # Accessibility
    has_wheelchair_access = detect_amenity(amenities, "Wheelchair accessible"),
    has_step_free = detect_amenity(amenities, "Step-free access"),
    
    # Family amenities
    has_family_friendly = detect_amenity(amenities, "Family/kid friendly"),
    has_high_chair = detect_amenity(amenities, "High chair"),
    has_crib = detect_amenity(amenities, "Crib"),
    
    # Guest convenience
    has_self_checkin = detect_amenity(amenities, "Self check-in"),
    has_lockbox = detect_amenity(amenities, "Lockbox"),
    
    # Policies (use cautiously - might be strategic responses to market)
    allows_pets = detect_amenity(amenities, "Pets allowed"),
    allows_smoking = detect_amenity(amenities, "Smoking allowed"),
    
    # Aggregated counts
    total_amenities = str_count(amenities, '","') + 1,
    
    premium_amenity_count = 
      has_pool + has_hot_tub + has_gym + has_elevator + 
      has_indoor_fireplace + has_waterfront + has_beachfront,
    
    essential_amenity_count = 
      has_wifi + has_kitchen + has_air_conditioning + 
      has_heating + has_washer + has_tv,
    
    safety_amenity_count = 
      has_smoke_detector + has_carbon_monoxide + 
      has_fire_extinguisher + has_first_aid,
    
    # Derived features
    has_any_parking = has_free_parking | has_street_parking | has_paid_parking,
    is_remote_work_ready = has_workspace & has_wifi,
    premium_ratio = premium_amenity_count / (total_amenities + 1)
  ) %>%
  select(-amenities)  # Remove original amenities string


# ============================================================================
# STEP 4: HANDLE HOST VERIFICATION COLUMNS
# ============================================================================

# Instead of 18+ individual verification columns, created summary metrics
verification_cols <- grep("host_verifications_", names(airbnb_clean), value = TRUE)

if (length(verification_cols) > 0) {
  airbnb_clean <- airbnb_clean %>%
    mutate(
      verification_count = rowSums(
        select(., all_of(verification_cols)) == "True" | 
        select(., all_of(verification_cols)) == TRUE,
        na.rm = TRUE
      ),
      has_government_id = if_else(
        host_verifications_government_id %in% c("True", TRUE), TRUE, FALSE
      ),
      has_jumio_verification = if_else(
        host_verifications_jumio %in% c("True", TRUE), TRUE, FALSE
      )
    ) %>%
    select(-all_of(verification_cols))
}


# ============================================================================
# STEP 5: FEATURE ENGINEERING (PRE-SPLIT)
# ============================================================================

airbnb_clean <- airbnb_clean %>%
  mutate(
    # Host tenure (experience indicator)
    host_since_date = as.Date(host_since, format = "%m/%d/%Y"),
    scrape_date = as.Date("2018-12-07"),  # Based on data
    host_tenure_days = as.numeric(scrape_date - host_since_date),
    host_tenure_years = host_tenure_days / 365.25,
    
    # Property capacity metrics
    guests_per_bedroom = accommodates / (bedrooms + 0.1),
    beds_per_bedroom = beds / (bedrooms + 0.1),
    bathrooms_per_guest = bathrooms / (accommodates + 0.1),
    
    is_flexible_stay = minimum_nights <= 2,
    accepts_long_stays = maximum_nights >= 365,
    
    # Host activity level (might indicate professionalism)
    is_multi_listing_host = calculated_host_listings_count > 1,
    host_listing_intensity = log1p(calculated_host_listings_count),
    
    # Response indicators (if host_response_rate exists)
    is_responsive_host = if_else(!is.na(host_response_rate) & host_response_rate >= 90, 
                                  TRUE, FALSE, missing = FALSE)
  ) %>%
  select(-host_since_date, -scrape_date, -host_tenure_days, -host_since)


# ============================================================================
# STEP 6: CONVERT CATEGORICAL VARIABLES TO FACTORS
# ============================================================================

# Convert character variables to factors for Random Forest
categorical_vars <- c(
  "property_type", "room_type", "bed_type", 
  "neighborhood", "cancellation_policy",
  "host_response_time"
)

airbnb_clean <- airbnb_clean %>%
  mutate(across(any_of(categorical_vars), as.factor))

# Handle high-cardinality categoricals
# If property_type has too many levels, group rare ones
if ("property_type" %in% names(airbnb_clean)) {
  property_freq <- airbnb_clean %>%
    count(property_type) %>%
    mutate(prop = n / sum(n))
  
  # Keep types that appear in >2% of listings, group others as "Other"
  common_types <- property_freq %>%
    filter(prop > 0.02) %>%
    pull(property_type)
  
  airbnb_clean <- airbnb_clean %>%
    mutate(property_type = if_else(property_type %in% common_types,
                                    as.character(property_type),
                                    "Other"),
           property_type = factor(property_type))
}


# ============================================================================
# STEP 7: REMOVE NEAR-ZERO VARIANCE PREDICTORS
# ============================================================================

# Identify features with no variance (all same value)
nzv_check <- nearZeroVar(airbnb_clean %>% select(-price_tier), 
                         saveMetrics = TRUE)
nzv_remove <- rownames(nzv_check[nzv_check$nzv == TRUE, ])

if (length(nzv_remove) > 0) {
  #cat("Removing", length(nzv_remove), "near-zero variance features:\n")
  #cat(paste(nzv_remove, collapse = ", "), "\n")
  
  airbnb_clean2 <- airbnb_clean %>%
    select(-any_of(nzv_remove))
}


# ============================================================================
# STEP 8: HANDLE EXTREME OUTLIERS
# ============================================================================

airbnb_clean2 <- airbnb_clean2 %>%
  mutate(
    # Cap unrealistic values
    minimum_nights = if_else(minimum_nights > 365, 365, minimum_nights),
    maximum_nights = if_else(maximum_nights > 365*10, 365*10, maximum_nights),
    
    # Cap extreme host listing counts (likely data errors or hotel chains)
    calculated_host_listings_count = if_else(
      calculated_host_listings_count > 50, 50, calculated_host_listings_count
    ),
    
    # Cap accommodates (listings claiming 16+ guests are unusual)
    accommodates = if_else(accommodates > 12, 12, accommodates)
  )

airbnb_clean2$neighborhood <- NULL
airbnb_clean3 <- feature_engineering(airbnb_clean2)

The first step was to remove variables that could lead to data leakage. For price tier prediction, the most critical concern is including features that are either direct representations of price or consequences of the pricing decision. The variables removed were price, weekly price, monthly price which would have allowed the model to cheat by learning the direct price rather than property characteristics. Other variables related to reviews which are post listing were also removed because they dont exist for new listings.

The original amenities column contained JSON-like strings with 17,476 unique combinations across 18,316 listings, making it impossible to use directly in machine learning models.We extracted 33 binary (TRUE/FALSE) amenity features based on: Frequency of appearance (>5% of listings), differentiation potential between price tiers,structural vs. operational characteristics (preferring permanent features) where we came up with 6 essential ammenities, 7 premium amenities, 3 parking amenities etc. We also aggregated the amenities metrics and created variables like total amenities among others. The dataset contained 18 separate host verification variables, wed decided rather than having 18 sparse variables we could consolidate them and create summary metrics like verification count because it captures all the trustworthiness without creating high dimensional sparse data.

We did data type standardization like boolean conversion: original data used text representations (“t”/“f”) for logical variables. All were converted to proper R logical types (TRUE/FALSE). Character variables were converted to factors and also handled high cardinality variables like property_type: we grouped rare categories (<2% frequency) into “Other”: Before: 34 property types (many with <10 listings), After: 7 categories (Apartment, House, Condominium, Townhouse, Serviced apartment, Other). We also created host tenure years which is (scrape date - host since date) with the rationale that more experienced hosts may price more accurately and offer higher quality. Also some variables related to host activity level and host responsiveness were engineered. Also created capacity ratios for the properties: these ratios capture property spaciousness and quality better than raw counts.

One of the feature selection and dimensionality reduction method we used is near-zero variance removal. In terms of handling outliers we used winsorization (capping) rather than removal to preserve all observations while limiting outlier influence.

3.3.0.1 Split the data

# ============================================================================
# STEP 10: STRATIFIED TRAIN/TEST SPLIT
# ===========================================================================

set.seed(42)  # For reproducibility
data_split <- initial_split(airbnb_clean3, prop = 0.75, strata = price_tier)

train_raw <- training(data_split)
test_raw <- testing(data_split)

cat("Training set:", nrow(train_raw), "rows\n")
cat("Test set:", nrow(test_raw), "rows\n")

# Verify stratification worked
cat("\nTraining set distribution:\n")
print(prop.table(table(train_raw$price_tier)))

cat("\nTest set distribution:\n")
print(prop.table(table(test_raw$price_tier)))

# 3) Compute imputers from *training* data only
train_medians <- compute_train_medians(train_raw)
train_modes   <- compute_train_modes(train_raw)

# 4) Apply imputation to both sets using training stats
df_train <- imputation(train_raw, train_medians, train_modes)
df_test  <- imputation(test_raw,  train_medians, train_modes)

#print(colSums(is.na(df_train)))
#print(sapply(df_train, class))
#print(colnames(df_train)[colSums(is.na(df_train)) > 0])

#write.csv(df_train, file = "df_train.csv", row.names = FALSE)
#write.csv(df_test, file = "df_test.csv", row.names = FALSE)


# save cleaned dataset for EDA
#airbnb_data <- clean(airbnb_data)
#all_medians <- compute_train_medians(airbnb_data)
#all_modes   <- compute_train_modes(airbnb_data)
#airbnb_data <- imputation(airbnb_data, all_medians, all_modes)
#airbnb_data <- feature_engineering(airbnb_data)
#write.csv(airbnb_data, file = "dataset_cleaned_2.csv", row.names = FALSE)

To ensure a robust evaluation, the data was then stratified and split into training (75%) and testing (25%) sets before any imputation.

All imputation values calculated from training data only, then applied to both training and test sets.We used median imputation because median is robust to outliers which is appropriate for skewed distributions and for categorical variables we used mode (most frequent category). Finally, we engineered new features by one-hot encoding of all categorical variables resulting in a clean and feature-rich dataset ready for the modeling phase.

4 Data Exploration and Visualization

4.1 Loading Datasets

  • Here we loaded both the cleaned and uncleaned datasets to visually understand the different results and challenges the uncleaned dataset has posed in regards to solving the classification problem.
library(tidyr)
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)

unclean_airbnb_df = read.csv("airbnb_data.csv")
airbnb_df <- read.csv("dataset_cleaned_2.csv")

4.2 Data Analysis

4.2.1 Data visualization

library(ggplot2)
library(visdat)
library(patchwork)
library(ggthemes)

unclean_airbnb_df$price_tier <- as.factor(unclean_airbnb_df$price_tier)

p1 <- ggplot(airbnb_df, aes(x = price_tier, fill=price_tier)) + 
  geom_bar()+
  labs(title="Distribution of the price tier (target variable)", x="Price Tier", y="Frequency") + theme_minimal()


p2 <- ggplot(airbnb_df, aes(x = price_tier, y = beds, fill = price_tier)) + geom_boxplot()+ 
  labs(title="Distribution of number of beds across price tier ", x="Price Tier", y="Number of beds") + theme_minimal() + theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14, hjust = 0.5),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 12)
  )

p1 + p2

- Here we see that the target class of our dataset are relatively balanced showing that there is an equal distribution of houses available to rent in the Airbnb Melbourne site. Another very informative plot is the boxplot that can be seen it shows the distribution of number of beds across the type of houses which shows that premium houses seem to have a higher median number of beds as compared to the other 2 types.

p3 <- ggplot(airbnb_df, aes(x = price_tier, y = accommodates, fill = price_tier)) + geom_boxplot()+ 
  labs(title="Variation in Accommodation Capacity Across Price Tiers", x="Price Tier", y="Accomodation capacity") + theme_tufte(base_size = 14, base_family = "serif") + theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14, hjust = 0.5),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 12)
  )


p4 <- ggplot(airbnb_df, aes(x = price_tier, y = cleaning_fee, fill = price_tier)) + geom_boxplot()+ 
  labs(title="Variation in cleaning fee Across Price Tiers", x="Price Tier", y="Cleaning fee") + theme_tufte(base_size = 14, base_family = "serif") + theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14, hjust = 0.5),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 12)
  )


p3 + p4

Variation in accommodation capacity and cleaning fees across price tiers. Budget listings typically accommodate 1–2 guests and have little or no cleaning fees. Standard listings accommodate slightly more guests and charge modest cleaning fees. Premium listings accommodate the largest number of guests (median ≈ 4) and show higher and more variable cleaning fees, with some extreme outliers. Overall, higher price tiers are associated with greater guest capacity and higher cleaning charges.

4.3 Analysis of Correlation between features

4.3.0.1 Uncleaned Dataset

library(corrplot)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(reshape2)

og_price_tier_unclean <- unclean_airbnb_df$price_tier
og_price_tier_clean <- airbnb_df$price_tier
unclean_airbnb_df$price_tier <- as.numeric(as.factor(unclean_airbnb_df$price_tier)) - 1
airbnb_df$price_tier <- as.numeric(as.factor(airbnb_df$price_tier)) - 1

num_features <- unclean_airbnb_df[sapply(unclean_airbnb_df, is.numeric)]
num_features <- num_features[, apply(num_features, 2, function(x) {
  var(x, na.rm = TRUE) > 0
})]
corr_mat1 <- cor(num_features, use="pairwise.complete.obs")
corr_mat1_mod <- abs(corr_mat1)
diag(corr_mat1_mod) <- 0
top_features <- names(sort(apply(corr_mat1_mod, 2, max), decreasing = TRUE))[1:30]
top_features <- c(top_features, "price_tier")
top_corr_mat1 <- corr_mat1[top_features, top_features]
corr_df_top1 <- melt(top_corr_mat1)

num_features1 <- airbnb_df[sapply(airbnb_df, is.numeric)]
corr_mat2 <- cor(num_features1, use="pairwise.complete.obs")
corr_mat2_mod <- abs(corr_mat2)
diag(corr_mat2_mod) <- 0
top_features <- names(sort(apply(corr_mat2_mod, 2, max), decreasing = TRUE))[1:27]
top_features <- c(top_features, "price_tier")
top_corr_mat2 <- corr_mat2[top_features, top_features]
corr_df_top2 <- melt(top_corr_mat2)

# Plot 1 - Before Cleaning
p1 <- ggplot(corr_df_top1, aes(Var1, Var2, fill = value)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "blue", mid = "white", high = "red", 
                       midpoint = 0, limit = c(-1, 1), name = "Correlation") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +  # base_size adjusts all text
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1, size = 8),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.ticks = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(title = "Correlated Features + Price Tier Before Cleaning", x = NULL, y = NULL) + coord_fixed()

# Plot 2 - After Cleaning
p2 <- ggplot(corr_df_top2, aes(Var1, Var2, fill = value)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "blue", mid = "white", high = "red", 
                       midpoint = 0, limit = c(-1, 1), guide = "none") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1, size = 8),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.ticks = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(title = "Correlated Features + Price Tier After Cleaning", x = NULL, y = NULL) + coord_fixed()

# Combine side by side
p1 + p2

airbnb_df$price_tier <- factor(airbnb_df$price_tier, 
                              levels = c(0, 1, 2),
                              labels = c("Budget", "Standard", "Premium"))
  • In the uncleaned dataset, many features exhibit weak correlations with one another, this makes interpretation of their relations often difficult to understand. This is most likely due to factors like missing values, inconsistencies or outliers, which can negatively distort the correlation estimates.
  • After the data cleaning and removal process for features with majority of its values missing, the correlation matrix has become a bit more interpretable. The remaining features display stronger correlations compared to the uncleaned heatmap, faciliating the identification of key patterns and aiding in dimensionality reduction techniques, such as PCA.
  • Features exhibiting correlation with the target variables are likely to be important predictors and should be treated with particular care, even when they are also correlated with other features. These influential features can be clearly seen to increase in the cleaned dataset as compared to the uncleaned one, where the top 20 correlated features are not that correlated to the target feature as compared to the top 20 features of the cleaned dataset.

4.3.1 PCA Analysis to understand Class Separation

library(ggfortify)
pca <- prcomp(num_features1, scale=TRUE)
autoplot(pca, data=airbnb_df, colour="price_tier") +
  ggtitle("PCA Projection of Features into 2 components")

- This PCA analysis highlights the difficulty in class separation. This visualization shows us clearly how all the three tier housing are extremely close to each other even while considering only 2 principal components, so simple linear algorithms like Logistic Regression and LDA may not be the most appropriate models to separate the classes from each other. This added complexity of the dataset further compels the dataset to undergo more cleaning other than just handling missing data such as outlier analysis, dimensionality reduction or handling missing data again using other machine learning models/bootstrap.

5 Modeling

This report presents a comprehensive comparison of five machine learning approaches for classifying Melbourne Airbnb listings into price tiers (Budget, Standard, Premium):

  1. Random Forest - Ensemble of decision trees
  2. XGBoost - Gradient boosted trees
  3. Multinomial Logistic Regression with Elastic Net - Regularized linear model
  4. k-Nearest Neighbors - Instance-based learning
  5. Support Vector Machine with PCA - Kernel-based with dimensionality reduction. And we will use Accuracy, F1-score, sensitivity, confusion matrix and specificity as some of our evaluation metrics together with hyperaparameter tuning and cross validation to get the best combination of parameters for the models.

Dataset: 13738 training observations, 4578 test observations, 124 features

5.1 Support Vector Machine

Nested Cross-Validation to find the best hyperparameters for support vector machine model - Having the outer folds of the nested cross validation for getting the mean accuracy of the model - The inner cross validation will be used to find the best hyperparameter (sigma and cost(C)) for the SVM model to perform at its best - The k-Folds architecture will be using a 5-fold CV architecture for the outer cross validation and a 3-fold CV architecture for the hyperparameter tuning of the SVM model

# If tuning results exist, skip model training and load directly
if (file.exists("svm_tune_res.rds")) {
  #cat("✅ Loading saved SVM tuning results...\n")
  svm_results <- readRDS("svm_tune_res.rds")
  final_svm <- svm_results$final_model
  tune_results <- svm_results$tune_results
  best.params <- svm_results$best_params
} else {
  #cat("⚙️ Running SVM hyperparameter tuning (first run — may take several minutes)...\n")
  
  outer_folds <- createFolds(process.train$price_tier, k = 5, list = TRUE, returnTrain = TRUE)
  tune_grid <- expand.grid(
    C = c(0.1, 1, 10),
    sigma = c(0.001, 0.01, 0.1)
  )
  
  for (i in seq_along(outer_folds)) {
    train_idx <- outer_folds[[i]]
    train_data <- process.train[train_idx, ]
    test_data  <- process.train[-train_idx, ]
    
    train_control <- trainControl(
      method = "cv",
      number = 3,
      allowParallel = TRUE
    )
    
    svm_tune <- train(
      price_tier ~ ., 
      data = train_data,
      method = "svmRadial",
      metric = "Accuracy",
      tuneGrid = tune_grid,
      trControl = train_control,
      preProcess = c("center", "scale"),
      tuneLength = 5
    )
    
    preds <- predict(svm_tune, newdata = test_data)
    cat("Fold", i, "completed.\n")
  }
  
  # Stop parallel backend
  stopCluster(cl)
  registerDoSEQ()
  
  tune_results <- svm_tune$results
  best.params <- svm_tune$bestTune
  
  # Fit final SVM with best params
  final_svm <- svm(
    price_tier ~ .,
    data = process.train,
    kernel = "radial",
    cost = best.params$C,
    gamma = best.params$sigma
  )
  
  svm_results <- list(
    tune_results = tune_results,
    best_params = best.params,
    final_model = final_svm
  )
  
  saveRDS(svm_results, file = "svm_tune_res.rds")
  #cat("💾 SVM tuning complete and saved to svm_tune_res.rds\n")
}
svm_results <- readRDS("svm_tune_res.rds")
final_svm <- svm_results$final_model
tune_results <- svm_results$tune_results
preds <- predict(final_svm, newdata = process.test)
plt1 <- ggplot(tune_results, aes(x = C, y = Kappa, color = factor(sigma))) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "SVM Hyperparameter Performance Comparision",
    x = "C (Regularization parameter)",
    y = "Kappa",
    color = "Sigma"
  )+
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +  # Reduced base size for grid layout
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 10),
    legend.title = element_blank(),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 9),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.line = element_line(color = "black"),
    panel.grid = element_blank()
  )

cm <- confusionMatrix(preds, process.test$price_tier)

cm_df <- as.data.frame(cm$table)

# Build results table
results_table <- data.frame(
  Class        = rownames(cm$byClass),
  Accuracy     = cm$overall["Accuracy"],
  Sensitivity  = cm$byClass[,"Sensitivity"],
  Specificity  = cm$byClass[,"Specificity"],
  Precision    = cm$byClass[,"Pos Pred Value"]
)

table <- ggplot() +
  annotation_custom(
    grob = tableGrob(
      results_table,
      rows = NULL
    )
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(
    title = "Classification Report"
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(
      size = 12, 
      hjust = 0.5,
      margin = margin(b = 5)
    ),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(
      size = 11,
      hjust = 0.5,
      margin = margin(b = 10)
    ),
    plot.margin = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
  )


plt2 <- ggplot(cm_df, aes(Prediction, Reference, fill = Freq)) +
  geom_tile() +
  geom_text(aes(label = Freq), color = "black", size = 5) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "blue") +
  labs(title = "SVM Confusion Matrix",
       x = "Predicted",
       y = "Actual") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=10) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 9),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 8),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 7)
  )

(plt1 | plt2) / (table) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "SVM Model Performance Summary",
    theme = theme(
      plot.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5)
    )
  )

From the metric table we find that when our model is tested on the test data that is processed using the training data we find the following information: * The SVM model with a C = 1 and gamma = 0.001, gives an accuracy of average 78% for all the three price tiers * We can also see how well the model performs for identifying each of the three price tiers - We see that the average sensitivity of the model on the test data is very high (averaging around 79%), implying that the model can identify the actual positives for each class correctly. But we see a major dip in sensitivity quality of the model for standard class data, this issue can be correlated with the idea implying in the data exploration section showing that some of the Premium/Budget properties may be considered as Standard property when it is not due to some correlated features that was missed out during data cleaning. - We see that the average specificity of the model on the test data is also very high (averaging 89%), impying that the model has a fairly good performance in determining actual negatives/incorrect classes of the training set correctly. - Overall we see that the model performs fairly decent in regards to overall model accuracy, precision and specificity of the model. * From the confusion matrix created, we can further understand the quality of our model, we see that the SVM model using a radial kernel is able to accurate predict the Budget, Premium and Standard priced houses but we also see that about 950 Standard houses were misclassified as Budget houses and 780 Premium houses were classified as Standard houses, this shows that some of the features have some non-linear correlation with each other that is not identified by the SVM model and hence were unable to create a proper boundary * But overall we see that the vast majority of the input test data was correctly identified as its appropriate price class.

5.2 Random Forest model

This analysis implements a Random Forest classifier to predict Airbnb price tiers (Budget, Standard, Premium) in Melbourne. Random Forest is an ensemble method that builds multiple decision trees and aggregates their predictions, providing robust performance and natural resistance to overfitting.

Target Distribution:

5.2.1 Cross-Validation Strategy

  • Used a 5-fold stratified cross-validation to maintain class balance and random seed (123) for reproducibility We also applied preprocessing steps for the workflow to be reproducible:
  1. Logical to numeric conversion: TRUE/FALSE → 1/0 (required for ranger). 2. Missing value imputation: Median for numeric, mode for categorical. 3. Zero-variance removal: Eliminate uninformative constant features. 4. Correlation filtering: Remove highly correlated features (r > 0.9). 5. Feature normalization: Standardize numeric predictors for consistent importance metrics. 6. Rare category grouping: Combine categories with <1% frequency

5.2.2 Model Specification

# Cross validation
set.seed(123)
cv_folds <- vfold_cv(df_train, v = 5, strata = price_tier)

# preprocess the data
rf_recipe <- recipe(price_tier ~ ., data = df_train) %>%
  step_rm(matches("^id$|^ID$"), skip = TRUE) %>%
  step_mutate(across(where(is.logical), as.numeric)) %>%
  step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) %>%
  step_impute_mode(all_nominal_predictors()) %>%
  step_zv(all_predictors()) %>%
  step_corr(all_numeric_predictors(), threshold = 0.9) %>%
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) %>%
  step_other(all_nominal_predictors(), threshold = 0.01, other = "other_combined") %>% 
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors())

# define your model
rf_spec <- rand_forest(
  mtry = tune(),
  trees = tune(),
  min_n = tune()
) %>%
  set_engine(
    "ranger",
    importance = "impurity",
    seed = 123,
    num.threads = 1
  ) %>%
  set_mode("classification")

# Create a workflow
rf_workflow <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(rf_recipe) %>%
  add_model(rf_spec)

# define the metrics
multiclass_metrics <- yardstick::metric_set(
  yardstick::accuracy,
  yardstick::roc_auc,
  yardstick::mn_log_loss,
  yardstick::sens,
  yardstick::spec,
  yardstick::precision,
  yardstick::f_meas
)

Hyperparameters tuned: - mtry: Number of features randomly sampled at each split (range: 10-30) - trees: Number of trees in the forest (range: 500-2,000) - min_n: Minimum observations in terminal nodes (range: 1-9). Tuning strategy: Latin hypercube sampling with 20 combinations for efficient parameter space coverage

# Check if tuning results already exist
if (file.exists("rf_tune_res.rds")) {
  #cat("✅ Loading saved tuning results...\n")
  rf_list <- readRDS("rf_tune_res.rds")
  rf_tune_results <- rf_list$tune_results
  rf_workflow <- rf_list$rf_workflow
} else {
  cat("⚙️ Running hyperparameter tuning (first time, may take a while)...\n")
  
  rf_grid <- grid_space_filling(
    parameters(
      mtry(range = c(5, 20)),
      trees(range = c(1000, 2000)),
      min_n(range = c(1, 9))
    ),
    size = 5,
    original = "lhs"
  )
  
  tune_control <- control_grid(
    save_pred = TRUE,
    save_workflow = FALSE,
    verbose = FALSE,
    allow_par = TRUE,
    parallel_over = "everything"
  )
  
  start_time <- Sys.time()
  set.seed(456)
  
  rf_tune_results <- tune_grid(
    rf_workflow,
    resamples = cv_folds,
    grid = rf_grid,
    metrics = multiclass_metrics,
    control = tune_control
  )
  
  rf_list <- list(
    tune_results = rf_tune_results,
    rf_workflow = rf_workflow
  )
  
  saveRDS(rf_list, file = "rf_tune_res.rds")
  end_time <- Sys.time()
  tuning_duration <- difftime(end_time, start_time, units = "mins")
  #cat("💾 Tuning complete and saved. Duration:", round(tuning_duration, 2), "mins\n")
}

Interpretation: - mtry = 16: Uses 16 features per split (out of ~124 total) - trees = 2000: Ensemble of 2000 decision trees - min_n = 5: Minimum 5 observations required to split a node

5.2.3 Hyperparameter Tuning Visualization

Key Findings: - Model performance varies significantly across different hyperparameter combinations - High number of trees (2000) suggests ensemble diversity is beneficial - Moderate mtry value indicates balanced feature sampling

final_rf_workflow <- finalize_workflow(rf_workflow, best_params)
final_rf_fit <- fit(final_rf_workflow, data = df_train)
final_rf_model <- extract_fit_parsnip(final_rf_fit)

test_predictions <- predict(final_rf_fit, df_test) %>%
  bind_cols(predict(final_rf_fit, df_test, type = "prob")) %>%
  bind_cols(df_test %>% select(price_tier))

# Calculate all metrics
test_accuracy <- yardstick::accuracy(test_predictions, truth = price_tier, estimate = .pred_class)
test_sensitivity <- yardstick::sensitivity(test_predictions, truth = price_tier, estimate = .pred_class, estimator = "macro")
test_specificity <- yardstick::specificity(test_predictions, truth = price_tier, estimate = .pred_class, estimator = "macro")
test_precision <- yardstick::precision(test_predictions, truth = price_tier, estimate = .pred_class, estimator = "macro")
test_f1 <- yardstick::f_meas(test_predictions, truth = price_tier, estimate = .pred_class, estimator = "macro")

prob_cols <- names(test_predictions)[stringr::str_detect(names(test_predictions), "^\\.pred_")]
prob_cols <- prob_cols[prob_cols != ".pred_class"]
test_roc_auc <- yardstick::roc_auc(test_predictions, truth = price_tier, all_of(prob_cols), estimator = "hand_till")

test_logloss <- yardstick::mn_log_loss(test_predictions, truth = price_tier, all_of(prob_cols))

5.2.4 ROC Curves and parameter tuning visualization

roc_data <- test_predictions %>%
  yardstick::roc_curve(truth = price_tier, all_of(prob_cols))

# autoplot(roc_data) +
#   labs(
#     title = "ROC Curves by Price Tier (One-vs-All)",
#     subtitle = paste0("Overall AUC (Hand & Till): ", round(test_roc_auc$.estimate, 4))
#   ) +
#   theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
#   theme(
#     plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
#     plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
#     legend.position = "bottom"
#   )

g3 <- roc_data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = 1 - specificity, y = sensitivity, color = .level)) +
  geom_path(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_abline(lty = 2, color = "gray50") +
  coord_equal() +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma", end = 0.9) +
  labs(
    title = "ROC Curves by Price Tier (One-vs-All)",
    subtitle = paste0("Overall AUC (Hand & Till): ", round(test_roc_auc$.estimate, 4)),
    x = "False Positive Rate (1 - Specificity)",
    y = "True Positive Rate (Sensitivity)",
    color = "Price Tier"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

g3+g1

ROC curves for each price tier using one-vs-all strategy. Curves closer to top-left indicate better discrimination. 

comparison <- tibble(
  Metric = c("ROC AUC", "Accuracy", "F1 Score"),
  `Cross-Validation` = c(
    show_best(rf_tune_results, metric = "roc_auc", n = 1)$mean,
    show_best(rf_tune_results, metric = "accuracy", n = 1)$mean,
    show_best(rf_tune_results, metric = "f_meas", n = 1)$mean
  ),
  `Test Set` = c(
    test_roc_auc$.estimate,
    test_accuracy$.estimate,
    test_f1$.estimate
  )
) %>%
  mutate(
    Difference = `Test Set` - `Cross-Validation`,
    `% Change` = round((Difference / `Cross-Validation`) * 100, 2),
    across(c(`Cross-Validation`, `Test Set`), ~round(., 4))
  )

# knitr::kable(
#  comparison,
#  caption = "Cross-Validation vs Test Set Performance Comparison")

g4 <- gridExtra::tableGrob(
  comparison,
  rows = NULL,
  theme = gridExtra::ttheme_minimal(
    core = list(fg_params = list(hjust = 0, x = 0.1)),
    colhead = list(fg_params = list(fontface = "bold"))
  )
)

# Add title to the table
g4 <- gridExtra::arrangeGrob(
  g4,
  top = grid::textGrob(
    "Cross-Validation vs Test Set Performance",
    gp = grid::gpar(fontsize = 12, fontface = "bold")
  )
)

5.2.4.1 Most Important Feature analysis and model generalization

g2 <- importance_scores %>%
  slice_max(order_by = Importance, n = 20) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Importance, y = reorder(Variable, Importance))) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Importance, 1)), hjust = -0.1, size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Top 20 Most Important Features",
    subtitle = "Random Forest - Gini Impurity Importance",
    x = "Importance Score",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank()
  ) +
  xlim(0, max(importance_scores$Importance[1:20]) * 1.1)


g2+wrap_elements(g4)

Variable importance based on mean decrease in Gini impurity. Higher values indicate more influential features.

Generalization Assessment: ✓ Excellent generalization - CV and test performance are nearly identical (<2% difference). The Random Forest model performs slightly better on the test set compared to cross-validation, with a 0.56% difference in ROC AUC.

5.2.5 Model Performance Summary

The Random Forest classifier achieved: - 76.3% overall accuracy on the test set - 0.91 ROC AUC indicating excellent discrimination ability - 0.765 F1 score demonstrating balanced precision and recall - 0.6% difference between CV and test performance (strong generalization)

5.3 K Nearest Neighbors

library(tidymodels)
library(yardstick)
library(gridExtra)
library(patchwork)

# load preprocessed data
train = read.csv('train.csv')
test = read.csv('test.csv')

# convert target column to factor
train$price_tier <- as.factor(train$price_tier)
test$price_tier <- as.factor(test$price_tier)

# standardization: important for knn as knn is sensitive to scale
rec <- recipe(price_tier ~ ., data = train) %>%
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())


# ================================================
#               HYPERPARAMETER TUNING
# ================================================

# Define model and workflow
knn_def <- nearest_neighbor(
  mode = 'classification',
  neighbors  = tune(),
  dist_power  = tune(),
  weight_func = "rectangular" # unweighted -> standard kNN
) %>% 
  set_engine("kknn")

knn_wf <- workflow() %>%
  add_model(knn_def) %>%
  add_recipe(rec)

# Cross-validation folds
set.seed(5003)
cv_folds <- vfold_cv(train, v = 5, strata = price_tier)

# Define weighted metrics for multiclass classification
f_meas_weighted <- metric_tweak(
  .name = "f_meas_weighted",
  .fn   = yardstick::f_meas,
  estimator = "macro_weighted"
)

precision_weighted <- metric_tweak(
  .name = "precision_weighted",
  .fn   = yardstick::precision,
  estimator = "macro_weighted"
)

recall_weighted <- metric_tweak(
  .name = "recall_weighted",
  .fn   = yardstick::recall,
  estimator = "macro_weighted"
)

multiclass_metrics <- metric_set(
  yardstick::accuracy,
  yardstick::sens,
  yardstick::spec,
  yardstick::precision,
  yardstick::f_meas,
  f_meas_weighted,
  precision_weighted,
  recall_weighted
)

# Define parameter grid
param_grid <- tidyr::crossing(
  neighbors  = seq(5, 50, 5),
  dist_power = c(1, 2)
)

ctrl <- control_grid(save_pred = TRUE, verbose = TRUE)

# ------------------------------------------------
#        Load or perform tuning
# ------------------------------------------------

if (file.exists("knn_tune_res.rds")) {
  #cat("✅ Loading saved KNN tuning results...\n")
  knn_tune_res <- readRDS("knn_tune_res.rds")
} else {
  #cat("⚙️ Running KNN hyperparameter tuning (this may take a few minutes)...\n")
  
  knn_tune_res <- tune_grid(
    knn_wf,
    resamples = cv_folds,
    grid      = param_grid,
    metrics   = multiclass_metrics,
    control   = ctrl
  )
  
  saveRDS(knn_tune_res, file = "knn_tune_res.rds")
  #cat("💾 Tuning results saved to knn_tune_res.rds\n")
}




# ================================================
#                PLOT TUNING RESULTS
# ================================================

m <- collect_metrics(knn_tune_res)

if ("dist_power" %in% names(m)) {
  m <- m %>%
    mutate(dist_power = factor(dist_power,
                               levels = c(1, 2),
                               labels = c("L1 (p=1)", "L2 (p=2)")))
}

res_plot <- m %>%
  filter(.metric %in% c('f_meas_weighted')) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = neighbors, y = mean,
             color = if ('dist_power' %in% names(m)) dist_power else NULL)) +
  facet_wrap(~ .metric, scales = 'free_y') +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean - std_err, ymax = mean + std_err), width = 0.6) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = sort(unique(m$neighbors))) +
  labs(
    title = 'knn: CV Weighted F1-Score',
    x = 'k (neighbors)',
    y = 'CV mean \u00B1 SE',
    color = 'Distance'
  ) +
  theme_minimal()


# ================================================
#     FINAL MODEL (HIGHEST WEIGHTED F1 SCORE)
# ================================================

if (file.exists("best_knn.rds")) {
  #cat("✅ Loading saved final KNN model...\n")
  best_knn <- readRDS("best_knn.rds")
} else {
  #cat("⚙️ Training final KNN model...\n")
  best_params <- select_best(knn_tune_res, metric = 'f_meas_weighted')
  best_knn_wf <- finalize_workflow(knn_wf, best_params)
  best_knn <- fit(best_knn_wf, data = train)
  saveRDS(best_knn, file = "best_knn.rds")
  #cat("💾 Final KNN model saved to best_knn.rds\n")
}



# ================================================
#     PLOTS TO EVALUATE FINAL MODEL PERFORMANCE
# ================================================

# Check if saved predictions exist
if (file.exists("best_knn_pred.rds")) {
  #message("Loading saved predictions...")
  best_knn_pred <- readRDS("best_knn_pred.rds")
} else {
  #message("Generating predictions and saving results...")
  best_knn_pred <- predict(best_knn, test) %>%
    bind_cols(test)
  saveRDS(best_knn_pred, file = "best_knn_pred.rds")
}

# Generate confusion matrix
cm <- conf_mat(best_knn_pred, truth = price_tier, estimate = .pred_class)

# Convert confusion matrix to a tidy data frame
cm_df <- as.data.frame(cm$table)
colnames(cm_df) <- c("Predicted", "Actual", "Freq")


# plot confusion matrix
cm_plot <- ggplot(cm_df, aes(x = Actual, y = Predicted, fill = Freq)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", , linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = Freq), size = 3.5, color = "black", fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "#1E90FF") +
  labs(
    title = "Confusion Matrix",
    x = "Actual Price Tier",
    y = "Predicted Price Tier",
    fill = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +  # Reduced base size for grid layout
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold", size = 16),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 9),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 8),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 7)
  )



# classification report table
overall_acc <- sum(cm_df$Freq[cm_df$Predicted == cm_df$Actual]) / sum(cm_df$Freq)
class_levels <- levels(test$price_tier)

report_table <- map_dfr(class_levels, function(cls) {
  TP <- cm_df$Freq[cm_df$Actual == cls & cm_df$Predicted == cls]
  FN <- sum(cm_df$Freq[cm_df$Actual == cls & cm_df$Predicted != cls])
  FP <- sum(cm_df$Freq[cm_df$Actual != cls & cm_df$Predicted == cls])
  Support <- sum(cm_df$Freq[cm_df$Actual == cls])

  Precision <- TP / (TP + FP)
  Recall    <- TP / (TP + FN)
  F1        <- 2 * (Precision * Recall) / (Precision + Recall)

  tibble(
    Class = cls,
    Precision = Precision,
    Recall = Recall,
    F1 = F1,
    Support = Support
  )
})

macro_row <- report_table %>%
  summarise(
    Class = "Macro Avg",
    Precision = mean(Precision),
    Recall    = mean(Recall),
    F1        = mean(F1),
    Support   = sum(Support)
  )

weighted_row <- report_table %>%
  summarise(
    Class = "Weighted Avg",
    Precision = weighted.mean(Precision, w = Support),
    Recall    = weighted.mean(Recall, w = Support),
    F1        = weighted.mean(F1, w = Support),
    Support   = sum(Support)
  )

# save for result table
wp_knn <- weighted.mean(report_table$Precision, w = report_table$Support)
wr_knn <- weighted.mean(report_table$Recall, w = report_table$Support)
wf_knn <- weighted.mean(report_table$F1, w = report_table$Support)

# merge result into a single table
final_report <- bind_rows(report_table, macro_row, weighted_row) %>%
  mutate(across(c(Precision, Recall, F1), ~ round(.x, 4)))

display_df <- final_report %>%
  mutate(
    Precision = sprintf("%.4f", Precision),
    Recall    = sprintf("%.4f", Recall),
    F1        = sprintf("%.4f", F1)
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(Class, Precision, Recall, F1, Support)


row_n  <- nrow(display_df)
row_bg <- c(rep(c("#F8F9FA", "#FFFFFF"), length.out = max(row_n - 2, 0)),
            rep("#E8F4F8", min(2, row_n)))   # last two rows shaded

table_theme <- ttheme_minimal(
  base_size = 10,
  core = list(
    fg_params = list(hjust = 0.5, x = 0.5),
    bg_params = list(fill = row_bg)
  ),
  colhead = list(
    fg_params = list(fontface = "bold", fontsize = 11, col = "white"),
    bg_params = list(fill = "#1E90FF")
  ),
  rowhead = list(fg_params = list(hjust = 0.5, x = 0.5))
)


tbl_grob <- tableGrob(display_df, rows = NULL, theme = table_theme)
tbl_grob$widths <- unit(rep(1, ncol(display_df)), "null")


classification_report_plot <- ggplot() +
  annotation_custom(
    grob = tbl_grob,
    xmin = -Inf, xmax = Inf, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(
    title    = "Classification Report",
    subtitle = paste("Overall Accuracy:", sprintf("%.4f", overall_acc))
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, margin = margin(b = 5)),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, margin = margin(b = 10)),
    plot.margin   = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
  )



# ================================================
#                COMBINE VISUALIZATIONS
# ================================================
(res_plot | cm_plot) / classification_report_plot +
  plot_layout(heights = c(1, 2), guides = "collect")

The K-Nearest Neighbors model was optimized using cross-validation on weighted F1-score. The tuning process evaluated different neighborhood sizes (k) and distance metrics (L1 and L2). The results show that performance improves as k increases and stabilizes around k ≥ 25, with L1 distance consistently outperforming L2. The best-performing configuration selected by cross-validation is k = 45 and L1 distance. The final model achieved 71.1% overall accuracy, with Budget predicted most reliably, Premium showing high precision but lower recall, and Standard being the most difficult class due to frequent confusion with Budget and Premium.

5.4 Multinomial Logistic Regression

This analysis implements multinomial logistic regression with elastic net regularization for predicting price tiers. The model uses tidymodels framework with parallel processing for hyperparameter tuning.

5.4.1 Cross-Validation Setup and hyperparameter tuning

Here the model is tuned to find optimal hyperparameter using the

set.seed(123)
cv_folds <- vfold_cv(df_train, v = 5, strata = price_tier)

mlr_recipe <- recipe(price_tier ~ ., data = df_train) %>%
  step_rm(matches("^id$|^ID$"), skip = TRUE) %>%
  step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) %>%
  step_impute_mode(all_nominal_predictors()) %>%
  step_nzv(all_predictors(), freq_cut = 95/5, unique_cut = 10) %>%
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) %>%
  step_corr(all_numeric_predictors(), threshold = 0.9) %>%
  step_other(all_nominal_predictors(), threshold = 0.01, other = "other") %>%
  step_integer(all_nominal_predictors()) %>%
  step_zv(all_predictors())

mlr_spec <- multinom_reg(
  penalty = tune(),
  mixture = tune()
) %>%
  set_engine("glmnet") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

mlr_workflow <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(mlr_recipe) %>%
  add_model(mlr_spec)


mlr_grid <- grid_regular(
  penalty(range = c(-4, 1)),
  mixture(range = c(0, 1)),
  levels = c(penalty = 10, mixture = 5)
)

# For multiclass classification, use yardstick metrics only
# Use only yardstick metrics explicitly to avoid caret conflicts
multiclass_metrics <- yardstick::metric_set(
  yardstick::accuracy,
  yardstick::roc_auc,
  yardstick::mn_log_loss,
  yardstick::sens,
  yardstick::spec,
  yardstick::precision,
  yardstick::f_meas)


tune_control <- control_grid(
  save_pred = TRUE,
  save_workflow = FALSE,
  verbose = TRUE,
  allow_par = TRUE,
  parallel_over = "everything",
  extract = NULL
)

start_time <- Sys.time()
set.seed(456)

mlr_tune_results <- tryCatch({
  tune_grid(
    mlr_workflow,
    resamples = cv_folds,
    grid = mlr_grid,
    metrics = multiclass_metrics,
    control = tune_control
  )
}, error = function(e) {
  cat("\nTuning error:", conditionMessage(e), "\n")
  return(NULL)
})

end_time <- Sys.time()
tuning_duration <- difftime(end_time, start_time, units = "mins")

if (is.null(mlr_tune_results)) {
  stopCluster(cl)
  registerDoSEQ()
  stop("Tuning failed. Check error messages.")
}

5.4.2 Performance by Regularization Parameters

best_params <- select_best(mlr_tune_results, metric = "roc_auc")
final_mlr_workflow <- finalize_workflow(mlr_workflow, best_params)
final_mlr_fit <- fit(final_mlr_workflow, data = df_train)

final_mlr_fit <- fit(final_mlr_workflow, data = df_train)

5.4.3 Test Set Performance

# Get test predictions (class + probabilities)
test_predictions <- predict(final_mlr_fit, df_test) %>%
  bind_cols(predict(final_mlr_fit, df_test, type = "prob")) %>%
  bind_cols(df_test %>% select(price_tier))

# Compute metrics (using yardstick explicitly to avoid caret conflicts)
test_accuracy <- yardstick::accuracy(test_predictions, truth = price_tier, estimate = .pred_class)
test_sensitivity <- yardstick::sensitivity(test_predictions, truth = price_tier, estimate = .pred_class, estimator = "macro")
test_specificity <- yardstick::specificity(test_predictions, truth = price_tier, estimate = .pred_class, estimator = "macro")
test_precision <- yardstick::precision(test_predictions, truth = price_tier, estimate = .pred_class, estimator = "macro")
test_f1 <- yardstick::f_meas(test_predictions, truth = price_tier, estimate = .pred_class, estimator = "macro")

# Get probability columns for multiclass ROC AUC
prob_cols <- names(test_predictions)[stringr::str_detect(names(test_predictions), "^\\.pred_")]
prob_cols <- prob_cols[prob_cols != ".pred_class"]

test_roc_auc <- yardstick::roc_auc(test_predictions, truth = price_tier, all_of(prob_cols), estimator = "macro")

# Combine all metrics into a single summary table
test_metrics <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  test_accuracy,
  test_sensitivity,
  test_specificity,
  test_precision,
  test_f1,
  test_roc_auc
) %>%
  dplyr::select(.metric, .estimate) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(desc(.estimate))

# Display the summary
#print(test_metrics)

# Confusion matrix (heatmap visualization)
test_conf_mat <- yardstick::conf_mat(test_predictions, truth = price_tier, estimate = .pred_class)

p5 <- autoplot(test_conf_mat, type = "heatmap") +
  labs(
    title = "Test Set Confusion Matrix",
    subtitle = paste("Accuracy:", round(test_accuracy$.estimate, 4))
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size=10) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 9),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 8),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 7)
  )

# Round estimates for readability
metrics_table <- test_metrics %>%
  arrange(desc(.estimate)) %>%
  mutate(.estimate = round(.estimate, 3))

# Create ggplot table
table_plot <- ggplot() +
  annotation_custom(
    grob = tableGrob(
      metrics_table,
      rows = NULL,          # removes row names
      theme = ttheme_default(
        core = list(fg_params = list(cex = 0.8)),   # text size
        colhead = list(fg_params = list(cex = 0.9, fontface = "bold"))
      )
    )
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Classification Report") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(
      size = 12, 
      hjust = 0.5,
      margin = margin(b = 5)
    ),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(
      size = 11,
      hjust = 0.5,
      margin = margin(b = 10)
    ),
    plot.margin = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
  )

# Print the table
( p1 | p5 | table_plot) +
  plot_layout(ncol = 3, widths = c(1, 1, 1)) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "MultiClass Logisitic Regression Model Performance Summary",
    theme = theme(
      plot.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold", hjust = 1)
    )
  )

Here, we have selected the optimal Regularized Multiclass Logistic Regression model by tuning the model using Nested Cross Validation: - The tuning architecture used had 5 outer folds to evaluate model performance and 5 inner folds to select optimal hyperparameters. - Tuned penalty (regularization strength) and alpha (regularizer type) using nested cross-validation. - Optimal hyperparameters found: penalty = 1e-4, alpha = 0.5 → elastic net regularization.

  • Here, we see from the metrics table that even with the optimal hyperparameter and regularization, we see an accuracy of only 71% and a f1-score of 71% for the test data. This shows that the model does perform modestly but can be improved on even further.
  • We also see that the model has a specificity of 85%, which shows that the model performs decently when identifying false classes for a test input, but falters to a 71% sensitivity when it has to correctly identify the correct class for the test data. This shows that the model is not a confident price tier identifier for new data, but it is a good false positive alarm.

5.5 XGBoost Model Implementation

  • We trained the model on the top 15 features and see from the validation loss curve (plateauing) that the model is not overfitting. From the confusion matrix we see expectedly, that model confuses less between budget and premium category. The Recall, Precision and F1 scores are strong showing good confidence in model’s predictive power.
# ============================================================================
# SETUP AND CONFIGURATION
# ============================================================================
# Load required libraries for modeling, visualization, and evaluation
library(xgboost)                    # Gradient boosting framework
library(dplyr)                      # Data manipulation
library(ggplot2)                    # Visualization
library(tidyr)                      # Data reshaping
library(caret)                      # Confusion matrix and evaluation metrics
library(gt)                         # Publication-ready tables
library(scales)                     # Color scales for plots
library(ParBayesianOptimization)    # Bayesian hyperparameter tuning
library(gridExtra)                  # Load gridExtra for table visualization
library(patchwork)                  # Combine multiple plots

# Set random seed for reproducibility across all operations
set.seed(42)

# ============================================================================
# DATA LOADING AND PREPROCESSING
# ============================================================================
# Load training dataset from CSV file
df <- read.csv("df_train_sub.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# Separate target variable (price_tier) from predictor features
# Ensure target is a factor and trim any whitespace
target <- factor(trimws(df$price_tier))
features <- df %>% select(-price_tier)

# Convert to numeric matrices required by XGBoost
# XGBoost requires numeric labels starting from 0
X <- as.matrix(features)
y <- as.numeric(target) - 1  # Convert factor levels (1,2,3...) to (0,1,2...)

# Create DMatrix object - XGBoost's internal data structure for efficiency
dtrain <- xgb.DMatrix(data = X, label = y)

# ============================================================================
# FEATURE IMPORTANCE ANALYSIS
# ============================================================================
# Configuration: Number of top features to display in visualization
TOP_N_FEATURES <- 15

# Train a preliminary model to assess feature importance
# This is a quick model with minimal rounds to get initial feature rankings
quick_model <- xgb.train(
  data = dtrain,
  booster = "gbtree",                              # Tree-based boosting
  objective = "multi:softprob",                    # Multi-class classification with probabilities
  num_class = length(unique(y)),                   # Number of target classes
  nrounds = 50,                                    # Moderate number of iterations
  eval_metric = "mlogloss",                        # Multi-class log loss
  verbose = 0                                      # Suppress training output
)

# Extract feature importance scores based on information gain
# Gain: Total reduction in loss when feature is used for splitting
importance_matrix <- xgb.importance(
  feature_names = colnames(X), 
  model = quick_model
)

# Select top N most important features for visualization
importance_df <- importance_matrix[1:TOP_N_FEATURES, ]
# Reverse factor levels for proper ordering in horizontal bar chart
importance_df$Feature <- factor(
  importance_df$Feature, 
  levels = rev(importance_df$Feature)
)

# Create horizontal bar chart of feature importances
p1 <- ggplot(importance_df, aes(x = Feature, y = Gain, fill = Gain)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +  # Horizontal bars for better label readability
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#FFDDC1", high = "#FF5733") +
  labs(
    title = paste("Top", TOP_N_FEATURES, "Feature Importances"),
    x = "Feature",
    y = "Importance (Gain)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +  # Reduced base size for grid layout
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, size = 16),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 9),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 8),
    panel.grid = element_blank()
  )
