# ============================================================================
# FEATURE SELECTION
# ============================================================================
# Define final feature set for model training
# Selected based on domain knowledge and importance analysis
train_features <- c(
  "room_type",                    # Type of accommodation
  "bedrooms",                     # Number of bedrooms
  "longitude",                    # Geographic coordinate
  "accommodates",                 # Maximum guest capacity
  "latitude",                     # Geographic coordinate
  "availability_flexibility",     # Booking flexibility score
  "review_stability",             # Review consistency metric
  "bathrooms",                    # Number of bathrooms
  "cleaning_fee",                 # Cleaning fee amount
  "extra_people",                 # Fee per additional guest
  "property_type",                # Property category
  "calendar_updated",             # Recency of calendar updates
  "amenities",                    # Number/quality of amenities
  "number_of_reviews",            # Total review count
  "guests_included"               # Base number of included guests
)

# Extract selected features and recreate matrices
features <- df[, train_features]
X <- as.matrix(features)

# Recreate DMatrix with reduced feature set
dtrain <- xgb.DMatrix(data = X, label = y)

# ============================================================================
# HYPERPARAMETER OPTIMIZATION (BAYESIAN OPTIMIZATION)
# ============================================================================
# Define objective function for Bayesian optimization
# This function evaluates a set of hyperparameters via cross-validation
# Returns: negative log loss (for maximization) and optimal number of rounds
scoringFunction <- function(eta, max_depth, gamma, colsample_bytree, 
                           min_child_weight, subsample) {
  
  # Progress tracking during optimization
  cat("Testing: eta =", eta, "| max_depth =", max_depth, "\n")
  flush.console()

  # Configure XGBoost parameters for current evaluation
  params <- list(
    booster = "gbtree",                            # Tree-based model
    objective = "multi:softprob",                  # Multi-class probabilities
    num_class = length(unique(y)),                 # Number of classes
    eval_metric = "mlogloss",                      # Evaluation metric
    eta = eta,                                     # Learning rate (shrinkage)
    max_depth = as.integer(max_depth),            # Maximum tree depth
    gamma = gamma,                                 # Min loss reduction for split
    colsample_bytree = colsample_bytree,          # Feature sampling per tree
    min_child_weight = min_child_weight,          # Min sum of weights in leaf
    subsample = subsample,                         # Row sampling per tree
    nthread = 6                                    # Parallel threads
  )

  # Perform 3-fold stratified cross-validation
  cv <- xgb.cv(
    params = params,
    data = dtrain,
    nrounds = 300,                                 # Max boosting rounds
    nfold = 3,                                     # Number of CV folds
    stratified = TRUE,                             # Maintain class distribution
    verbose = 0,                                   # Suppress output
    early_stopping_rounds = 3                      # Stop if no improvement
  )

  # Return negative log loss (Bayesian opt maximizes, we want to minimize loss)
  list(
    Score = -min(cv$evaluation_log$test_mlogloss_mean),
    nrounds = cv$best_iteration
  )
}

# Define hyperparameter search space bounds
bounds <- list(
  eta = c(0.01, 0.05),                    # Learning rate range
  max_depth = c(3L, 6L),                  # Tree depth range
  gamma = c(0, 5),                        # Regularization parameter
  colsample_bytree = c(0.5, 1.0),        # Feature sampling ratio
  min_child_weight = c(2, 5),            # Minimum leaf weight
  subsample = c(0.5, 1.0)                # Row sampling ratio
)

# Execute Bayesian optimization
# Uses Gaussian Process to intelligently explore hyperparameter space
opt_result <- bayesOpt(
  FUN = scoringFunction,
  bounds = bounds,
  initPoints = 10,                        # Random initialization points
  iters.n = 20,                           # Optimization iterations
  acq = "ei"                              # Expected Improvement acquisition
)

# Extract and display best hyperparameters found
best_params <- getBestPars(opt_result)
print(best_params)

# Save optimization results for future use
saveRDS(opt_result, file = "opt_result_sub.rds")

# ============================================================================
# FINAL MODEL TRAINING
# ============================================================================
# Load previously optimized hyperparameters
opt_result <- readRDS("opt_result_sub.rds")
best_params <- getBestPars(opt_result)

# Create train/validation split (80/20)
set.seed(42)
train_idx <- sample(seq_len(nrow(X)), size = 0.8 * nrow(X))

# Split features and labels
X_train <- X[train_idx, ]
y_train <- y[train_idx]
X_val   <- X[-train_idx, ]
y_val   <- y[-train_idx]

# Create DMatrix objects for training and validation sets
dtrain <- xgb.DMatrix(data = X_train, label = y_train)
dval   <- xgb.DMatrix(data = X_val, label = y_val)

# Set up watchlist to monitor performance on both sets during training
watchlist <- list(train = dtrain, eval = dval)

# Train final model with optimized hyperparameters
final_model <- xgb.train(
  params = list(
    booster = "gbtree",
    objective = "multi:softprob",
    num_class = length(unique(y)),
    eval_metric = "mlogloss",
    eta = best_params$eta,
    max_depth = best_params$max_depth,
    gamma = best_params$gamma,
    colsample_bytree = best_params$colsample_bytree,
    min_child_weight = best_params$min_child_weight,
    subsample = best_params$subsample
  ),
  data = dtrain,
  nrounds = 300,                          # Maximum training rounds
  watchlist = watchlist,                  # Track train and validation metrics
  verbose = 1,                            # Show progress
  print_every_n = 100                     # Print every 100 rounds
)

# Save trained model to disk
xgb.save(final_model, "xgb_trained_model.model")

# ============================================================================
# TRAINING VISUALIZATION
# ============================================================================
# Extract training history from model
log_df <- as.data.frame(final_model$evaluation_log)

# Identify best iteration (minimum validation loss)
best_iter <- which.min(log_df$eval_mlogloss)
best_loss <- log_df$eval_mlogloss[best_iter]

# Detect plateau point (no improvement for 10 consecutive rounds)
# This helps identify when early stopping should have occurred
plateau_iter <- NA
for (i in seq(best_iter, nrow(log_df) - 10)) {
  if (all(log_df$eval_mlogloss[i:(i + 9)] > log_df$eval_mlogloss[i - 1])) {
    plateau_iter <- i
    break
  }
}

# Create learning curve plot
p2 <- ggplot(log_df, aes(x = iter)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = train_mlogloss, color = "Train"), linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_line(aes(y = eval_mlogloss, color = "Validation"), linewidth = 0.8) +
  # Mark best iteration with vertical line
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = best_iter,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "darkgreen",
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  # Highlight best point
  geom_point(
    data = data.frame(iter = best_iter, loss = best_loss),
    aes(x = iter, y = loss),
    color = "darkgreen",
    size = 2
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = best_iter, y = best_loss,
    label = paste("Best:", best_iter),
    hjust = -0.1, vjust = 1.2,
    color = "darkgreen",
    size = 3
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Training vs Validation Log Loss",
    x = "Iteration",
    y = "Log Loss"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Train" = "#FF5733", "Validation" = "#1E90FF")) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +  # Reduced base size for grid layout
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, size = 16),
    legend.title = element_blank(),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 9),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.line = element_line(color = "black"),
    panel.grid = element_blank()
  )

# ============================================================================
# MODEL EVALUATION ON TEST SET
# ============================================================================
# Load trained model from disk
loaded_model <- xgb.load("xgb_trained_model.model")

# Load test dataset
test_df <- read.csv("df_test_sub.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# Extract same features used during training (critical for consistency)
features_test <- test_df[, train_features]
X_test <- as.matrix(features_test)

# Prepare true labels from test set
target_test <- factor(trimws(test_df$price_tier))
y_test <- as.numeric(target_test) - 1

# Generate predictions on test set
dtest <- xgb.DMatrix(data = X_test)
pred_probs <- predict(loaded_model, dtest)

# Convert probability vector to class predictions
# pred_probs is returned as a flat vector, reshape to (n_samples x n_classes)
n_classes <- length(levels(target_test))
pred_matrix <- matrix(pred_probs, ncol = n_classes, byrow = TRUE)
pred_class <- max.col(pred_matrix) - 1  # Get class with highest probability

# Convert numeric predictions back to original factor labels
pred_labels <- factor(
  levels(target_test)[pred_class + 1], 
  levels = levels(target_test)
)

# Generate confusion matrix and classification metrics
cm <- confusionMatrix(pred_labels, target_test)

# ============================================================================
# CONFUSION MATRIX VISUALIZATION
# ============================================================================
# Convert confusion matrix to data frame for plotting
cm_table <- as.data.frame(cm$table)
colnames(cm_table) <- c("Predicted", "Actual", "Freq")

# Create heatmap visualization of confusion matrix
p3 <- ggplot(cm_table, aes(x = Actual, y = Predicted, fill = Freq)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = Freq), size = 3.5, color = "black", fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "#1E90FF") +
  labs(
    title = "Confusion Matrix - Test Set",
    x = "Actual Price Tier",
    y = "Predicted Price Tier",
    fill = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +  # Reduced base size for grid layout
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold", size = 16),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 9),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 8),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 7)
  )

# ============================================================================
# DETAILED CLASSIFICATION REPORT
# ============================================================================
# Extract confusion matrix as numeric matrix
cm_matrix <- as.matrix(cm$table)

# Set class labels for rows and columns
true_classes <- levels(target_test)
rownames(cm_matrix) <- true_classes
colnames(cm_matrix) <- true_classes

# Calculate per-class performance metrics
# For each class, compute Precision, Recall, and F1-Score
metrics_list <- lapply(true_classes, function(cls) {
  # Extract True/False Positives/Negatives for current class
  TP <- cm_matrix[cls, cls]                      # Correctly predicted as class
  FP <- sum(cm_matrix[cls, ]) - TP               # Incorrectly predicted as class
  FN <- sum(cm_matrix[, cls]) - TP               # Missed predictions for class
  TN <- sum(cm_matrix) - TP - FP - FN            # Correctly predicted as other
  
  # Calculate classification metrics
  Precision <- ifelse((TP + FP) == 0, 0, TP / (TP + FP))  # Correct among predicted
  Recall    <- ifelse((TP + FN) == 0, 0, TP / (TP + FN))  # Correct among actual
  F1        <- ifelse((Precision + Recall) == 0, 0,       # Harmonic mean
                     2 * Precision * Recall / (Precision + Recall))
  Support   <- TP + FN                                     # Total samples in class
  
  data.frame(
    Class = cls, 
    Precision = Precision, 
    Recall = Recall, 
    F1 = F1, 
    Support = Support
  )
})

# Combine per-class metrics into single data frame
metrics_df <- bind_rows(metrics_list)

# Calculate overall accuracy
total_support <- sum(metrics_df$Support)
overall_accuracy <- sum(diag(cm_matrix)) / sum(cm_matrix)

# Calculate macro average (unweighted mean across classes)
macro_avg <- metrics_df %>%
  summarise(
    Precision = mean(Precision),
    Recall = mean(Recall),
    F1 = mean(F1),
    Support = total_support
  ) %>%
  mutate(Class = "Macro Avg")

# Calculate weighted average (weighted by class support)
weighted_avg <- metrics_df %>%
  summarise(
    Precision = sum(Precision * Support) / total_support,
    Recall = sum(Recall * Support) / total_support,
    F1 = sum(F1 * Support) / total_support,
    Support = total_support
  ) %>%
  mutate(Class = "Weighted Avg")

wp_subhamay <- sum(metrics_df$Precision * metrics_df$Support) / total_support
wr_subhamay <- sum(metrics_df$Recall * metrics_df$Support) / total_support
wf_subhamay <- sum(metrics_df$F1 * metrics_df$Support) / total_support

# Combine all metrics into final report
report_df <- bind_rows(metrics_df, macro_avg, weighted_avg) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(., 4)))

# Format the data for display
display_df <- report_df %>%
  mutate(
    Precision = sprintf("%.4f", Precision),
    Recall = sprintf("%.4f", Recall),
    F1 = sprintf("%.4f", F1)
  )

# Create theme for table
table_theme <- ttheme_minimal(
  base_size = 10,
  core = list(
    fg_params = list(hjust = 0.5, x = 0.5, fontface = "plain"),
    bg_params = list(
      fill = c(rep(c("#F8F9FA", "#FFFFFF"), length.out = nrow(display_df) - 2),
               "#E8F4F8", "#E8F4F8")  # Light blue for summary rows
    )
  ),
  colhead = list(
    fg_params = list(fontface = "bold", fontsize = 11),
    bg_params = list(fill = "#1E90FF", col = "white")
  ),
  rowhead = list(
    fg_params = list(hjust = 0.5, x = 0.5, fontface = "plain")
  )
)

# Convert to ggplot table
p4 <- ggplot() +
  annotation_custom(
    grob = tableGrob(
      display_df,
      rows = NULL,
      theme = table_theme
    )
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(
    title = "Classification Report",
    subtitle = paste("Overall Accuracy:", round(overall_accuracy, 4))
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(
      size = 16, 
      face = "bold", 
      hjust = 0.5,
      margin = margin(b = 5)
    ),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(
      size = 11,
      hjust = 0.5,
      face = "bold",
      margin = margin(b = 10)
    ),
    plot.margin = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
  )

# ============================================================================
# COMBINED VISUALIZATION GRID
# ============================================================================
# Combine all visualizations into 2x2 grid using patchwork
# Layout: Feature Importance | Training Curves
#         Confusion Matrix   | Classification Report
(p1 | p2) / (p3 | p4) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "XGBoost Model Performance Summary",
    theme = theme(
      plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5)
    )
  )

6 Results

We evaluated five machine learning algorithms to answer our research question: To what extent can machine learning identify key differentiators between Budget, Standard, and Premium Airbnb listings in Melbourne?

# Create a table comparing 5 models
model_eval <- tibble(
  Model = c("KNN", "SVM", "Random Forest", "MultiClass Logisitic Regression", "XGBoost"),
  
  # Hyperparameters as a string for readability
  Hyperparameters = c(
    "neighbors=45, dist_powe=1",
    "C=1, gamma=0.001, kernel='rbf'",
    "mtry=16, trees = 2000, min_n = 5",
    "alpha=0.01, l1_ratio=0.5",
    hyperparameters_subhamay
  ),
  
  Accuracy = c(0.710, 0.780, 0.763, 0.711, 0.762),
  F1_Score    = c(0.710, 0.765, 0.766, 0.715, 0.763),
  ROC_AUC   = c(0.825, 0.856, 0.910, 0.866, 0.904)
)

# Round metrics for readability
model_eval <- model_eval %>%
  mutate(
    Accuracy = round(Accuracy, 3),
    F1_Score    = round(F1_Score, 3),
    ROC_AUC        = round(ROC_AUC, 3)
  )

# Create GT table
model_eval %>%
  gt() %>%
  fmt_number(
    columns = vars(Accuracy, F1_Score, ROC_AUC),
    decimals = 3
  ) %>%
  cols_label(
    Model = "Model",
    Hyperparameters = "Hyperparameters",
    Accuracy = "Accuracy",
    F1_Score = "F1-Score",
    ROC_AUC = "ROC_AUC"
  ) %>%
  tab_options(
    table.font.size = 11,
    data_row.padding = px(4)
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels(everything())
  ) %>%
  opt_align_table_header("center")
Model Hyperparameters Accuracy F1-Score ROC_AUC
KNN neighbors=45, dist_powe=1 0.710 0.710 0.825
SVM C=1, gamma=0.001, kernel='rbf' 0.780 0.765 0.856
Random Forest mtry=16, trees = 2000, min_n = 5 0.763 0.766 0.910
MultiClass Logisitic Regression alpha=0.01, l1_ratio=0.5 0.711 0.715 0.866
XGBoost eta=0.05, max_depth=6.00, gamma=0.00, colsample_bytree=0.90, min_child_weight=4.14, subsample=0.50 0.762 0.763 0.904

Machine learning models successfully identified price tier differentiators with a classification accuracy of 78 percent. The Support Vector Machine (SVM) achieved the best overall performance, reaching 78.0 percent accuracy. The Random Forest model exhibited the strongest discrimination capability with a 91.0 percent ROC AUC, indicating excellent ability to distinguish between tiers, and also provided the most balanced performance with a 76.6 percent F1-score, reflecting a solid precision-recall balance. All models substantially outperformed the random guessing baseline of 33.3 percent for three classes. Overall, the 78 percent accuracy demonstrates that machine learning can reliably identify price tier patterns, highlighting its potential for practical real-world applications. The performance gap between the SVM (78 percent accuracy) and k-NN (71 percent accuracy) further suggests that capturing non-linear relationships and effectively handling high-dimensional data are key factors in achieving superior results.

6.0.1 Conclusion

The most influential factors in determining price tiers are primarily property-related, accounting for 81 percent of the model’s overall importance. The top three property characteristics are room type (entire home versus private room), capacity features such as the number of guests accommodated, bedrooms, and beds, and pricing structure elements including cleaning fees and guest policies. Interestingly, host characteristics—such as tenure, superhost status, and response rate—contribute only 5 percent to total importance. This finding suggests that new hosts can effectively compete in premium market segments by prioritizing property quality and amenities rather than relying on long-term experience or reputation.

Machine learning successfully answers our research question: We can identify price tier differentiators with 78% accuracy, and the most influential drivers are property capacity, room type, and amenities—not host experience. This finding democratizes the premium market for new hosts and provides actionable insights for Australia’s $4.8B short-term rental industry. The practical implication is clear: hosts should invest in property quality (bedrooms, amenities, capacity) rather than waiting years to build reputation. For a competitive market facing -12% occupancy declines in Sydney, these data-driven insights enable strategic decision-making that can directly impact revenue optimization.

6.0.2 Limitations and Future work

This study has several limitations that should be noted. Its geographic scope is restricted to a single city, Melbourne, which means the findings may not generalize to other cities such as Sydney or to regional areas. The temporal scope covers only one point in time (December 2018), so seasonal patterns and market fluctuations are not captured. Review sentiment was not incorporated, which would require natural language processing; and neighborhood-level details such as walkability or transit accessibility were unavailable.

Future work can address these gaps through several directions. Expanding the geographic scope to cities like Sydney and Brisbane would help assess generalizability, while analyzing multi-year data could reveal seasonal and longitudinal market trends. More advanced extensions include applying deep learning (using convolutional neural networks) to extract visual quality signals from property photos, using NLP-based sentiment analysis on reviews and descriptions, implementing causal inference via A/B testing to measure the true return on investment of amenity enhancements, and adopting dynamic modeling frameworks to update price tier predictions in real time based on market demand.

7 Appendices

7.1 References

OpenML. (n.d.). Data set with ID 46807 [Data set]. OpenML. Retrieved September 21, 2025, from https://www.openml.org/search?type=data&sort=runs&id=46807&status=active

TylerX. (n.d.). Melbourne Airbnb open data [Data set]. Kaggle. Retrieved September 21, 2025, from https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/tylerx/melbourne-airbnb-open-data

Inside Airbnb. (n.d.). Inside Airbnb: Adding data to the debate. Retrieved September 21, 2025, from https://insideairbnb.com/

Shi, X., Mueller, J., Erickson, N., Li, M., & Smola, A. J. (2021). Benchmarking multimodal AutoML for tabular data with text fields. arXiv. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2111.02705

7.2 Data Dictionary of cleaned dataset

library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(tidymodels)
library(tibble)


airbnb_data <- read.csv("dataset_cleaned.csv")

data_dict <- data.frame(
  Type = sapply(airbnb_data, function(x) base::class(x)[1]),
  # Description = "",  # You can fill this manually if desired
  Range = sapply(airbnb_data, function(x) {
    if(is.numeric(x)) {
      paste0(min(x, na.rm = TRUE), " - ", max(x, na.rm = TRUE))
    } else if(is.factor(x) || is.character(x)) {
      paste(head(unique(x), 5), collapse = ", ")  # show first 5 unique values
    } else {
      ""
    }
  }),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# Display nicely
kable(data_dict) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
Type Range
id integer 9835 - 30593623
host_response_time character within an hour, a day or more, within a few hours
host_is_superhost logical
host_has_profile_pic logical
host_identity_verified logical
neighborhood character St Kilda, Central Business District, Richmond, Carlton North, Southbank
property_type character Apartment, House, Condominium, Townhouse, Loft
room_type character Entire home/apt, Private room, Shared room
accommodates integer 1 - 16
bathrooms numeric 0 - 14
bedrooms integer 0 - 16
beds integer 0 - 24
bed_type character Real Bed, Pull-out Sofa, Airbed, Futon, Couch
price integer 0 - 12501
security_deposit integer 0 - 6950
cleaning_fee integer 0 - 867
guests_included integer 1 - 16
extra_people integer 0 - 421
minimum_nights integer 1 - 1125
maximum_nights integer 1 - 99999999
availability_30 integer 0 - 30
availability_60 integer 0 - 60
availability_90 integer 0 - 90
availability_365 integer 0 - 365
instant_bookable logical
cancellation_policy character flexible, strict_14_with_grace_period, moderate, super_strict_30, super_strict_60
require_guest_profile_picture logical
require_guest_phone_verification logical
calculated_host_listings_count integer 1 - 98
price_label integer 0 - 9
E_detail_len integer 166 - 4493
E_house_rules integer 1 - 1000
E_transit_near_southern_cross logical
E_transit_free_tram_zone logical
E_transit_airport_bus logical
E_transit_tram_nearby logical
E_transit_train_nearby logical
E_transit_bus_nearby logical
E_transit_uber_taxi logical
E_transit_parking_available logical
E_transit_walk_proximity logical
E_has_private_access logical
E_has_pool logical
E_has_kitchen logical
E_has_wifi logical
E_has_linen logical
E_host_present logical
E_self_checkin logical
E_full_privacy logical
E_concierge_available logical
E_contactable logical
E_total_amenities integer 1 - 94
E_review_span_m integer 1 - 101
E_review_volume numeric 0.01 - 535.44
E_review_scores_rating_x_response numeric 0 - 100
E_host_tenure_y numeric 6.77260273972603 - 16.6246575342466
E_host_response_rate integer 0 - 100
E_host_num_verifications integer 0 - 12
E_host_has_social_verif logical
E_host_has_govt_verif logical
E_calendar_updated_d integer 0 - 2490 
# -------------------------------
# Summary statistics
# -------------------------------
num_samples <- nrow(airbnb_data)
num_features <- ncol(airbnb_data) - 1  # exclude target
num_price_categories <- length(unique(airbnb_data$price_label))
num_numeric <- sum(sapply(airbnb_data, is.numeric))
num_factor <- sum(sapply(airbnb_data, is.factor))

overview_df <- data.frame(
  Metric = c("Number of Samples", "Number of Features", "Price Categories",
             "Numeric Columns", "Factor Columns"),
  Value = c(num_samples, num_features, num_price_categories, num_numeric, num_factor)
)

# Display table
kable(overview_df)
Metric Value
Number of Samples 18316
Number of Features 60
Price Categories 10
Numeric Columns 28
Factor Columns 0 
# -------------------------------
# Summarize Range and Unique Values
# -------------------------------
summary_report <- data.frame(
  data_type = sapply(airbnb_data, base::class),
  range = NA,
  unique_values = NA,
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# Fill in values
for (i in seq_along(airbnb_data)) {
  col <- airbnb_data[[i]]
  col_type <- base::class(col)
  unique_vals <- sort(unique(col))
  
  # Logical columns
  if (is.logical(col)) {
    summary_report$range[i] <- "0-1"
    summary_report$unique_values[i] <- length(unique_vals)
    
  # Numeric columns
  } else if (is.numeric(col) || is.integer(col)) {
      summary_report$range[i] <- paste0(min(col, na.rm = TRUE), "-", max(col, na.rm = TRUE))
      summary_report$unique_values[i] <- length(unique_vals)
  
  # Character/factor columns
  } else if (is.factor(col) || is.character(col)) {
    summary_report$range[i] <- NA
    summary_report$unique_values[i] <- length(unique_vals)
  }
}

# Display nicely
kable(summary_report)
data_type range unique_values
id integer 9835-30593623 18316
host_response_time character NA 3
host_is_superhost logical 0-1 2
host_has_profile_pic logical 0-1 2
host_identity_verified logical 0-1 2
neighborhood character NA 59
property_type character NA 34
room_type character NA 3
